coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent sensing platform for glutathione based on Förster resonance energy transfer and aggregation-induced emission

AIEE
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis conditions of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) and (B) are TEM and size distribution of N-CDs, respectively; (C) and (D) are TEM and size distribution of LA@Au NCs, respectively.
(A) Absorption spectra of N-CDs and Zn@N-CDs; (B) Excitation and emission spectra of Zn@N-CDs; (C) Emission wavelength dependence on excitation wavelength.
(A) Absorption spectra of LA@Au NCs; (B) Excitation and emission spectra of LA@Au NCs; (C) Emission wavelength dependence on excitation wavelength.
(A) Overlayed fluorescence spectra of Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs after addition of different amounts of GSH (0.1–90 µM) while (B) is calibration plot.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs fluorescent probe in presence of 25 µM GSH, 500 µM ions, and 350 µM biomolecules/organic and amino acids. Selectivity/anti-interference studies of Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs fluorescent probe towards 25 µM GSH and 500 µM ions (B) and 350 µM biomolecules (C).
Preparation of Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs and its utility for GSH detection.
Intraday and interday precision of the Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs for detecting GSH (n = 5).
Analysis of real samples containing GSH (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent sensing platform for glutathione based on Förster resonance energy transfer and aggregation-induced emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526 Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut 71526 Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451 Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh 11451 Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE UK

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), an amino-thiol compound consisting of cysteine, glycine, and glutamine linked through an amide-gamma bond, is considered a crucial biomolecule within the human body [1,2]. It plays significant roles in protecting living cells and maintaining normal metabolic processes [3,4]. GSH is involved in various vital functions, including detoxification, integration, and anti-oxidation [5,6]. Changes in GSH blood concentration are associated with serious health conditions such as atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, HIV, Parkinson's disease, and diabetes [7,8]. Accurate and reliable determination of GSH levels in biological samples is crucial for understanding its role in disease progression and for the potential development of new diagnostic tools. Conventional methods for determining GSH include electrochemistry [9,10], chromatography [11,12], electrophoresis [13,14], and spectrophotometry [15]. Among these methods, fluorometric techniques are particularly notable for being sensitive, simple, rapid, selective, and economical for the analysis of biomolecules [16–18].

Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention in sensing due to their stability, water solubility, strong fluorescence, and easy modification [19,20]. These properties make them ideal for applications in imaging, catalysis, drug delivery, and sensing [21,22]. Heteroatom doping e.g. N, B, and S enhances their photoluminescence properties, solubility, and surface modification potential [23,24].

Ratiometric-based fluorometric sensors have been widely applied in various fields, including environmental monitoring, clinical diagnosis, biochemistry, and molecular biology. This technique facilitates the detection of target analytes, even in complex matrices, by taking the ratio of two signals rather than measuring absolute values [25–27].

In this study, we introduce a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) for highly selective and sensitive GSH detection. This approach leverages a hybrid nanoplatform consisting of zinc and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Zn@N-CDs) and α-lipoic acid-functionalized gold nanoclusters (LA@Au NCs). The key mechanisms governing this system involve: (i) the interaction between Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs leads to fluorescence enhancement via AIEE and simultaneous quenching via FRET. (ii) The presence of GSH disrupts both AIEE and FRET processes due to its strong coordination with Zn2+ , resulting in a distinct fluorescence shift that enables accurate quantification. (iii) The ratiometric detection strategy enhances measurement precision by normalizing the fluorescence signal, reducing background noise, and improving selectivity in complex biological samples.

Glutathione (GSH, 98.8 %), moxifloxacin (97.8 %), dopamine (97.9 %), ascorbic acid (97.9 %), uric acid (97.8 %), glucose (96.7 %), serine (97.5 %), glutamic acid (98.6 %), aspartic acid (97.4 %), methionine (98.3 %), phenylalanine (98.2 %), citric acid (96.4 %), vitamin B6 (98.4 %), vitamin B12 (99.2 %), histidine (98.5 %), human serum albumin (HAS, 99.4 %), leucine (97.6 %), glycine (98.8 %), gold chloride (HAuCl4 ·3H2O, 99.4 %), α-lipoic acid (98.6 %), and cysteine (97.5 %), HCl, NaOH, methanol, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, acetonitrile, and zinc chloride hexahydrate (ZnCl2 ·6H2O) were procured from Sigma Aldrich.
Details for instruments used for measurements and characterization were outlined in supporting information.

9.15 mg of α-lipoic acid, serving as a stabilizer and reducing agent, was added to 4.8 mL NaOH: H2O mixture (2:8, v/v) and sonicated until completely dissolved. Subsequently, 6.8 mL of HAuCl4•3H2O (3.05 mM) was added with continuous stirring. Finally, the mixture was heated in an oven at 150 °C for 50 min, followed by freeze-drying to obtain a lyophilized powder.

To prepare Zn@N-CDs, 25.0 mg of moxifloxacin and 100.0 mg of ZnCl2 ·6H2O were solubilized in 60 mL of double-distilled water and sonicated for 20 min. Then, it was then heated at 190 °C for 12 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. Afterward, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min, followed by filtration and dialysis (MWCO: 2000 Da). To obtain lyophilized powder, the yellow-colored solution was freeze-dried.

450 µL of 0.22 mg/mL Zn@N-CDs was blended with 450 µL of 0.66 mg/mL LA@Au NCs and 750 µL of phosphate buffer solution (pH 6.0). Subsequently, it allowed standing at room temperature for three minutes. Then, 350 µL of varying concentrations of GSH was added, followed by incubation at room temperature for an additional five minutes. Fluorescence readings were taken after excitation at 320 nm (Scheme 1 ).

Dietary supplements containing capsules (average of one capsule) were dispersed in 50 mL double distilled water and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, followed by filtration. For biological samples (saliva, serum, and urine), 550 µL of 0.23 mg/mL Zn@N-CDs was mixed with 450 µL of 0.65 mg/mL LA@Au NCs and 650 µL of phosphate buffer solution. Following incubation at 3 min, 350 µL of biological fluid was added, followed by incubation at room temperature for an additional 5 min. Fluorescence readings were taken after excitation at 320 nm.

Different characterization tools were employed to demonstrate the successful synthesis of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs. Fig.S1
A shows the FT-IR spectra of α-lipoic acid (LA) and LA-functionalized Au NCs (LA@Au NCs). The spectra reveal that LA exhibits absorption bands at 3365, 2805–2870, 1704, and 1240 cm−1, corresponding to the υ(OH), υ(CH2), υ(C=O), and υ(S-S), respectively. Additionally, the absorption band at 1560 cm−1 corresponds to δ(OH) group in LA. These bands shift to lower wavenumbers upon functionalization with Au NCs [5]. Fig.S1
B displays the FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and Zn@N-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs shows absorption bands at 3450, 3310, 2907, and 1685 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH, CH2 , and C=O, respectively [36,37]. The absorption band at 1585 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibrations of OH/NH groups in CDs. Upon attaching Zn2+ , these bands exhibit blue shifts, indicating good coordination interaction between Zn2+ and the functional groups of CDs. Additionally, a new absorption band was appeared in the spectrum of Zn@N-CDs at 610 cm−1, which may be attributed to the stretching vibration of N-Zn-O. Fig.S1
C showcases the FT-IR of the LA@Au NCs/Zn@N-CDs hybrid. Fig. 1
A and B demonstrate the TEM image and size distribution of Zn@N-CDs. These nanoparticles exhibit a spherical shape with an average diameter of 3.4 ± 0.09 nm (n = 100) while Fig. 2
C and D display the TEM image and size distribution of LA@Au NCs, which have an average diameter of 2.2 ± 0.04 nm (n = 100). Fig.S2 demonstrates the PXRD patterns of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs. Fig. 2 A shows the PXRD pattern of Zn@N-CDs with a main diffraction peak at 21.8°, indicative of the graphitic-like structure of N-CDs [38,39]. Fig.S2
B displays the PXRD pattern of LA@Au NCs with main diffraction peaks at 24.9°, 36.8°, 53.2°, and 68.9°, corresponding to the (002), (111), (200), and (220) diffraction planes, respectively [40,41]. Fig.S3 explores the EDX patterns of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs. Fig.S3
A exhibits the EDX pattern of Zn@N-CDs, showing sharp peaks corresponding to C, N, O, and Zn, with percentages of 43.9 %, 33.2 %, 18.3 %, and 4.6 %, respectively. Fig.S3
B displays the EDX pattern of LA@Au NCs, showing peaks for C, O, S, and Au, with percentages of 56.5 %, 12.8 %, 6.7 %, and 24.0 %, respectively. Furthermore, the functional groups on the surface of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs were investigated using XPS ( Figs.S4
&S5). The XPS spectrum presented in Fig.S4 identifies the functional groups and binding energies in Zn@N-CDs. Sharp peaks are observed at 285.4 eV, 398.5 eV, 530.5 eV, and 1024.6 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and Zn 2p, respectively. The presence of two sharp deconvoluted peaks for Zn 2p at 1023.7 eV and 1044.8 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 , suggests the presence of both Zn⁰ and Zn2+ in Zn@N-CDs [41,42]. Fig.S5 represents the XPS spectrum of LA@Au NCs, showing observed peaks at 85.6 eV, 162.5 eV, 285.8 eV, and 529.4 eV, which correspond to Au 4f, S 2p, C 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The stability of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs was assessed under various conditions, including different temperatures, irradiation times, pH levels, and ionic strengths ( Figs.S6 & S7 ). The results demonstrated that both Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs exhibit a high degree of stability across these diverse conditions. The only notable observation was that fluorescence readings decreased with increasing temperature. As a result, room temperature (25 °C ± 2°C) was selected as the optimal temperature for further measurements.

Spectroscopic characterizations of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs are shown in Figs. 2 and 3 . The UV/Vis absorption spectrum of Zn@N-CDs (Fig. 2 A) reveals two absorption bands at 242 and 335 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of C=C and the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N, respectively [43]. After reacting with Zn, the absorption band at 242 nm disappears, likely due to changes in the electronic structure, while the absorption band at 335 nm exhibits a slight blue shift, indicating an alteration in the nanoparticles' electronic structure. Fig. 2 B presents the excitation/emission spectra of Zn@N-CDs, with excitation and emission wavelengths at 320 and 465 nm, respectively. Fig. 2 C shows the excitation-dependent emission, where both the emission wavelengths and intensities increase with the excitation wavelength, which suggests variations in surface functionality and/or size on the Zn@N-CDs surface [44]. Fig. 3 A shows the absorption spectra of LA@Au NCs, where the lack of an absorption band in the 350–700 nm suggests the absence of nanoparticles [45]. Fig. 3 B presents the excitation/emission spectra of LA@Au NCs, with excitation and emission wavelengths observed at 320 nm and 670 nm, respectively. Fig. 3 C indicates that the emission wavelengths are independent of the excitation wavelengths. Fig.S8 shows the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, Zn@N-CDs, and LA@Au NCs at 465 nm. The results indicate that LA@Au NCs do not exhibit fluorescence in this region, while N-CDs display weak fluorescence compared to Zn@N-CDs. Upon interaction with Zn, the fluorescence emission of N-CDs increases, accompanied by a shift to a shorter wavelength. The quantum yields (QY) of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs were calculated using equation listed in Electronic Supplementary Information and were found to 23.45 and 17.56 %, respectively. The higher QY of Zn@N-CDs can be attributed to: (i) the heteroatom doping effect (Zn and N), which enhances electron density and passivates surface defects, leading to stronger fluorescence emission. (ii) the intrinsic photoluminescence of N-CDs, which exhibits excitation-dependent emission behavior due to the presence of multiple emissive surface states. Conversely, LA@AuNCs exhibited a lower QY, which is consistent with the weak fluorescence emission of small AuNCs. However, their fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced by AIE in the presence of Zn2+ . This enhancement is likely due to: (i) restricted intramolecular motion, reducing non-radiative energy loss. (ii) coordination interactions between Zn2+ and LA, stabilizing the excited-state energy levels of the nanoclusters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis conditions of Zn@N-CDs and LA@Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Optimizing synthesis conditions is crucial for enhancing the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe. Therefore, the key variables affecting the synthesis process were optimized before applying the fluorescent system for GSH detection. Fig.S9 illustrates the different conditions influencing the synthesis of Zn@N-CDs, showing that the optimal precursor ratio, synthesis temperature, and heating time are 1:4 (moxifloxacin: Zn 2+), 190 °C, and 12 h, respectively. Additionally, Fig.S10 presents the optimal synthesis conditions for LA@Au NCs, with a precursor ratio of 1:3 (Au: LA), a synthesis temperature of 150 °C, and a heating time of 50 min.

Various reaction conditions were optimized, including reaction time, pH value, and the mass ratio of Zn@N-CDs to LA@Au NCs ( Fig.S11 ). Fig.S11
A illustrates the influence of reaction time on the response ratio (F670/F465). The response ratio increased until it reached an optimum value at 5 min and then stabilized. This suggests that 5 min is the optimal reaction time for the interaction between GSH and the fluorophore. Fig.S11
B shows the effect of the mass ratio of Zn@N-CDs to LA@Au NCs on the fluorescence response for GSH detection. The highest fluorescence response was observed at a ratio of 1:3, making this the optimal mass ratio for detecting GSH. Fig.S11C illustrates the effect of pH value on GSH detection within the range of 4.0 to 10.0. The optimal pH value for detecting GSH was determined to be 6.0. In acidic conditions, amino groups' protonation inhibits the complexation between N-CDs and Zn2+ . Conversely, in alkaline conditions, Zn2+ ion precipitation negatively impacts the probe's performance. Therefore, a pH of 6.0 was selected as the ideal value for subsequent analysis.

Several metal cations, including Zn2+ ions, have the ability to aggregate thiolated nanoclusters, thereby enhancing the fluorescence emission of these nanoclusters [46,47]. The strong affinity between Zn2+ ions and the stabilizing agents on the surface of nanoclusters, such as GSH and LA, arises from the robust coordination interactions between the metal cations and amino and/or carboxyl groups [48]. Furthermore, the incorporation of metals into CDs is crucial for boosting fluorescence emission. This process is enabled by the existence of electron-donating groups like amino groups (Lewis base) on the CDs' surface, with the metal ion serving as an electron acceptor (Lewis acid) [49]. Fig.S12
A illustrates the fluorescence emissions of LA@Au NCs, Zn-LA@Au NCs, N-CDs/LA@Au NCs, Zn@N-CDs, and Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs. The introduction of Zn2+ ions notably enhanced the fluorescence of weakly fluorescent LA@Au NCs through the mechanism of AIEE. Interestingly, the emission spectrum of N-CDs remained consistent upon the addition of LA@Au NCs. However, the fluorescence emission of N-CDs at 465 nm showed improvement after being doped with Zn2+ ion (Zn@N-CDs). Moreover, the emission peak of LA@Au NCs (acceptor) at 670 nm was intensified upon combination with Zn@N-CDs (donor), attributed to the AIEE. Fig.S12
B displays the excitation-dependent emission characteristics, leading to the conclusion that varying the excitation wavelength had no impact on the emission wavelength. This observation implies the uniformity of surface functional groups on Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs. Fig.S12
C showcases the UV/Vis absorption spectra of the Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs nanohybrid. It was observed that augmenting the quantity of Zn@N-CDs led to enhanced absorption features, potentially linked to background scattering. This phenomenon hints at the aggregation of LA@Au NCs by Zn@N-CDs. Fig.S12
D presents the TEM image of LA@Au NCs post the introduction of Zn@N-CDs, revealing a particle size escalation from 2.2 nm to 86.4 nm, indicating the aggregation of LA@Au NCs. The interaction between LA@Au NCs and Zn@N-CDs was verified using Zeta potential measurements ( Fig.S13
A). I t was found that the Zeta potential of N-CDs is −17.78 mV, which is attributed to electron rich groups on the surface of N-CDs. The Zeta potential value was decreased upon doping of N-CDs with Zn2+ to approximately 2.35 mV. The Zeta potential of LA@Au NCs was found to be −8.78 mV as a result of electron rich groups on the surface of LA. Mixing of LA@Au NCs and Zn@N-CDs resulted in Zeta potential of −1.14 mV. This electrostatic interaction between LA@Au NCs and Zn@N-CDs facilitated the coordination interaction between Zn2+ on the surface of Zn@N-CDs and LA on the surface of LA@Au NCs. Fig.S13
B depicts the overlap between the UV/Vis spectrum of LA@Au NCs and the fluorescence spectra of Zn@N-CDs, indicating the possible involvement of IFE or FRET in quenching the fluorescence emission of LA@Au NCs. To investigate whether this mechanism is IFE or FRET, fluorescence lifetimes were measured (as shown in Fig.S13
C). The fluorescence lifetime of LA@Au NCs was measured at 10.67 ns, and this value increased to 15.65 ns after the introduction of Zn@N-CDs, suggesting the presence of FRET between LA@Au NCs and Zn@N-CDs [50,51].

The Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs probe was utilized for detecting GSH under optimized conditions. In Fig. 4
A, the fluorescence emission spectra of the fluorescent probe are displayed after introducing varying amounts of GSH within the linear range of 0.1–90 µM. An increase in the emission band at 465 nm, accompanied by a bathochromic shift, was observed, while the emission band at 670 nm decreased. These changes are likely attributed to the disruption of AIE and FRET caused by the coordination interaction between Zn2+ and GSH. Additionally, a slight bathochromic shift could be attributed to the weak binding of Zn2+ on the surface of N-CDs. A linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence response (F465/F670) and the concentration of GSH. The linear regression equation obtained was (F465/F670) = 0.34 + 0.017 [GSH] with a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.9987), Fig. 4 B. The LOD (S/N = 3) was found to be 0.03 µM. Table S1 showcases the detection of GSH using various fluorescent probes. The findings suggest that the proposed probe displays satisfactory sensitivity and reliability.

To verify the reliability of the proposed system, it was tested for detecting GSH at three concentration levels (low, medium, and high) (Table 1 ). The low relative standard deviations (RSDs) observed confirmed the system's precision.

“To assess the reproducibility of the Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs fluorescent probe, eight separate batches were synthesized under same conditions. The results demonstrated excellent consistency, with RSDs remaining below 3.56 %, validating the probe’s reliability for GSH detection. The selectivity of the Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs fluorescent probe was assessed by examining its response to common interfering ions (500 µM) and biomolecules/organic and amino acids (350 µM). Among the tested substances, only GSH caused a significant change in fluorescence, confirming the probe's selective sensitivity towards GSH detection (Fig. 5 ).

The Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs fluorescent probe was employed for GSH detection in real samples, including dietary supplements and human biological fluids. The recovery rates ranged from 98.4 % to 102.3 %, with RSD between 1.43 % and 4.02 %, demonstrating the probe’s accuracy. Furthermore, a comparison with the HPLC method validated the reliability of this fluorescent system for GSH detection in real-world applications (Table 2 ). Moreover, the calculated t-values were found to be lower than the tabulated t-value (n = 5, t = 2.776, two-tailed test) at a 95 % confidence level, indicating no significant difference between the two methods.

A novel fluorescence detection system for GSH was designed, featuring a reliable and selective approach. The fluorescent probe combines blue-emissive Zn@N-CDs with orange-emissive LA@Au NCs. The introduction of cationic Zn@N-CDs induces LA@Au NCs' aggregation through coordination interactions between the Zn2
+ ions on the surface of Zn@N-CDs (donor) and the LA on the surface of Au NCs, enhancing the weak fluorescence emission of LA@Au NCs (acceptor). Upon the addition of GSH, both AIEE and FRET are disrupted due to the coordination interaction between GSH and Zn2+ , resulting in the restoration of N-CDs fluorescence at 465 nm and a decrease in LA@Au NCs fluorescence at 670 nm. The Zn@N-CDs/LA@Au NCs fluorescent system presents several advantages, including high reliability, simplicity, rapid detection, and excellent selectivity. Its successful application in real sample analysis further demonstrates its effectiveness for GSH detection, yielding highly satisfactory results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for funding this work through the project number (RSP2025R457).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126131.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

J. Exp. Bot.,

Commun., 58 (2022), pp. 10524-10527.
